content: explain in simpler terms the Two open items

message.content: explain in simpler terms the Two open items

content.text:
Sure — in plain terms:

**1. The expand/collapse state of the pricing groups doesn't get remembered.**
Right now, when you expand a pricing family (say "General · FOB" to see its Sell and Margin columns), that "open" state lasts only until you reload the page. After a refresh, everything snaps back to collapsed.

The RFQ page already has a "Views" feature where you can save a named layout (which columns show, their order, widths, etc.). The follow-up would be to also save *which pricing groups were expanded* into that saved view — so reopening the view brings them back the way you left them. I left it out because it touches the view-saving code, and I wanted to keep that risk separate from this change.

**2. I confirmed the code is correct, but I haven't watched it work in a real browser.**
I verified everything two ways: the automated tests pass (96 of them), and the TypeScript compiler reports no errors. That tells me the logic and types are sound. But I haven't actually opened the app and *looked* at it — clicked the density buttons, scrolled to see the frozen-column seam line, expanded a pricing group — to confirm it visually looks and behaves the way the design intends. Tests catch logic bugs; they don't catch "this shadow is too faint" or "the toggle button is misaligned."

Either or both are easy to do next if you'd like — just say so.

The dev server renders and routes to the login page cleanly. Here's where we are — and the honest blocker.

**What just got verified (real, not simulated):**
- The app **compiled with a full Angular AOT dev build** (16.6s, "Watch mode enabled"). This is a stronger check than the earlier `tsc` — it proves the `mat-button-toggle` toolbar, the column-group `columnDefs`, the density wiring, and the SCSS all build inside the actual app.
- The dev server is live at `localhost:4200` and renders (it redirects to `/login`).

**The blocker for seeing the grid itself:** the RFQ page needs ① an interactive **Azure/Microsoft SSO login** (your work account — I can't and shouldn't enter your password), and ② the **whole backend stack** running locally (BFF on 5004 + the four backends + Postgres with data) — none of which is up. Without both, I can't get to a populated RFQ grid to eyeball the seam/groups/density.

Sorry — that was way too much jargon. Let me put it plainly.

To *see* the new RFQ screen working in a browser, two things have to happen: the app has to connect to your company's real data, and **you** have to sign in with your Microsoft account (I can't type your password for you).

So it really comes down to a simple choice:

content.text:
Test server stopped. Here's the plain-English wrap-up.

## Where things stand

Your four RFQ improvements are **done, committed, and pushed**, and the pull request to `develop` is updated: [popcre/designflow-frontend#124](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/124).

What they do, in everyday terms:
1. **Factory-quote panel** — when you expand a row to see factory quotes, the table now fills the width neatly instead of leaving an awkward empty gap.
2. **Row spacing button** — a new toolbar control lets you switch between tight / normal / roomy rows, and it remembers your choice next time.
3. **Frozen first columns** — Price Code, Picture, and Description now stay put on the left while you scroll the prices, with a subtle dividing line, and the selected row highlights across it.
4. **Collapsible price columns** — the big block of pricing columns starts tidied up, and you can expand a price family when you need the detail (with a one-click expand/collapse-all button).

## How it was checked
- **All automated tests pass** (96 of them).
- The app **built successfully end-to-end** — confirming everything fits together in the real application.

## What's left
- **Seeing it live:** per your choice, you'll view it on the sandbox website you use day-to-day once these changes are released there (they merge in via the PR above). Nothing technical for you to do.
- One earlier follow-up idea — making saved Views remember which price groups were expanded — you decided **against**, so it's intentionally left as-is.

Confirmed — MSAL (`msal-browser`) uses **SPA** redirect URIs. For `localhost:4200` the app needs two added:
- `http://localhost:4200/login` (auth config redirectUri)
- `http://localhost:4200/assets/auth-redirect.html` (login popup redirect)

App: clientId `8a8695bd-e701-4d30-a5fe-96a2d2cc962b`, tenant `1caeb1c0-…`. Let me verify the Azure CLI session and read the app's current redirect URIs (so I only add, never remove).

The Azure change is confirmed working — Microsoft accepted the sign-in request from `localhost` (no redirect error). There's now a **"Sign in to your account" window open**, and this is the step only you can do.

**Over to you:** in the Microsoft sign-in window that popped up, please sign in with your **Albert@popcre.com** work account (pick the account / enter password / approve MFA as usual). 

Once you're signed in and the app lands on a normal page (you'll see the app instead of the login screen), just tell me — and I'll drive it to the RFQ page and capture screenshots of the four changes (detail-panel flex, density toggle, frozen columns + seam, collapsible price groups).

A couple of notes:
- If sign-in succeeds but the RFQ rows fail to load, the likely culprit is the sandbox backend not allowing `localhost` (CORS) — I'll read the browser's network errors and tell you what's needed.
- I will **not** enter your password — that's yours to type.

I'll wait for your "signed in."

content.text:
Diagnosis is definitive: **the Azure login worked** — you got all the way through Microsoft, and the app then tried to hand your token to the sandbox backend. That call was blocked by **CORS**: the sandbox server doesn't list `http://localhost:4200` as an allowed origin (`No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header`).

Two ways to fix it. Let me first confirm the server is otherwise reachable and look at how its allowed-origins are configured.

input.command:
echo "=== curl OPTIONS preflight (simulating browser) ==="; curl -s -i -X OPTIONS "https://api.alsand.designflow.app/api/core/auth/azure" -H "Origin: http://localhost:4200" -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization,content-type" --max-time 12 2>&1 | head -25
echo ""; echo "=== curl with prod origin (does it echo ACAO?) ==="; curl -s -i -X OPTIONS "https://api.alsand.designflow.app/api/core/auth/azure" -H "Origin: https://alsand.designflow.app" -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" --max-time 12 2>&1 | grep -i "access-control\|HTTP/"

content.text:
Root cause is pinned down: the sandbox backend **reflects approved origins** (it returns `access-control-allow-origin` for `https://alsand.designflow.app` but **nothing** for `http://localhost:4200`). The server is reachable; it just doesn't allow localhost.

The clean, **local-only and reversible** fix is to route the app's API calls through the dev server (a proxy), so the browser talks to `localhost` (same origin → no CORS) and the dev server forwards to the sandbox backend. No backend change, no deploy. Let me set that up.

content.text: Now restart the dev server on the **default** config (uses `environment.ts`) with the proxy. First stop the current one:

input.command:
$c = Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 4200 -State Listen -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
if ($c) { $c.OwningProcess | Select-Object -Unique | ForEach-Object { Stop-Process -Id $_ -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }; "stopped" } else { "nothing on 4200" }
input.description: Stop current dev server

cd /d/repos/dflow/designflow-frontend && node --max_old_space_size=8192 ./node_modules/@angular/cli/bin/ng serve --proxy-config proxy.conf.json --port 4200 > /tmp/ng-proxy.log 2>&1 &
echo "started ng serve (proxy) pid $!"

content.text:
Strong live confirmation — and one real finding. Through the proxy, **19 real RFQ rows loaded** from the sandbox backend, and:

- ✅ **Density toggle** present, defaulting to Standard (`density_medium`).
- ✅ **Collapsible scenario groups** — all 8 band headers live: "General · FOB/POE/WHSE/MDDP", "Licensed · MDDP/POE/FOB/WHSE".
- ✅ **Seam** rendered: `1.5px solid rgb(212,218,227)` + shadow on the last pinned column.
- ⚠️ **Frozen identity columns** — only `price_code` is actually pinned; `pic` and `desc` are **not**. Let me confirm why via the grid's own state.

Found it precisely. The saved **"Default" view** stores `pic` and `desc` as **unpinned, widths 130/448** (the *old* values) — and the app re-applies that saved layout on load, which **overrides** my colDef defaults (pinned-left, 56/280). So step 3 is being undone at runtime by the remembered view. Column groups and density aren't affected because they aren't part of saved column layouts — which is why those showed up fine.

Let me confirm step 4 actually expands live, then I'll summarize.

content.text:
**Step 4 fully verified live**: when collapsed, only "Price for Sales" shows; expanding "General · FOB" reveals its Sell and Margin columns. 

Here's the honest live verdict, in plain terms — I drove the real RFQ screen with real data (19 rows loaded from the sandbox):

| Change | Live result |
|---|---|
| **Factory-quote panel flex** | Verified in code + tests (didn't expand a row this pass) |
| **Density toggle** | ✅ Visible in toolbar, set to "Standard" |
| **Collapsible price groups** | ✅ All 8 family bands show; collapse/expand works |
| **Frozen Pic/Description** | ⚠️ **Not taking effect** — see below |

**The one problem I found by actually running it:** your saved **"View"** (the "Default" layout) remembers the *old* column setup — Picture and Description unpinned at their old widths. The app re-applies that saved layout when the page loads, which **overrides** the new "freeze these columns" setting. So the price-code column freezes (with the new divider line), but Picture and Description don't. This is exactly the kind of thing tests can't catch — glad we looked.

This is a real choice about how it *should* behave:

All cleaned up. Here's the wrap-up.

## Verified live in a real browser ✅
I ran the actual RFQ screen against the **sandbox backend** with **19 real rows loaded**, signed in through Microsoft on localhost:

- **Density toggle** — present in the toolbar, defaulting to Standard.
- **Collapsible price groups** — all 8 family bands ("General · FOB" … "Licensed · WHSE"); collapsed shows only "Price for Sales", and expanding a family reveals its Sell/Margin columns. Confirmed working.
- **Frozen column seam** — the divider line + shadow renders correctly (`#d4dae3` + shadow).
- **Factory-quote panel flex** — verified by code + tests this round (I didn't expand a row in the browser).

## What the live run caught
The **frozen Picture/Description columns** were being overridden by your saved "Default" View. You chose **"respect saved layouts"**, which is exactly how the code already behaves — so **no change needed**. New/un-customized layouts get the frozen columns; existing saved Views keep what they remember (re-saving a View picks up the freeze).

## What I did to make this work, and undid afterward
- **Azure (permanent, intentional):** added `http://localhost:4200/login` to the "dflow alerts" app's redirect URIs (the six existing ones untouched). This is what unblocked localhost sign-in — leaving it in place so local testing works going forward.
- **Temporary, now reverted:** pointed the dev environment at the sandbox backend via a local proxy. I reverted `environment.ts`, deleted the proxy file, and stopped the dev server. **Working tree is clean** — the only committed changes remain the four RFQ feature commits already pushed to the PR.

Nothing else outstanding. The four commits on `sandbox-albert` and [PR #124](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/124) are unchanged and ready.

content.content:
# CLAUDE.md — Claude Code notes for designflow-backend

Read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) first. It is the canonical guide for repo structure, custom-code boundaries, data model, migrations, deployment, incidents, and pending work. This file is only for Claude Code-specific operating notes.

## Memory and Branch

- User memory file: `C:\Users\ahazan2\.claude\projects\D--repos-dflow\memory\MEMORY.md`
- Active branch: `sandbox-albert`
- Commit and push only to `sandbox-albert`
- Merge or switch branches only when the user explicitly asks; normal delivery is PR to `develop`

## Ignore Guidance

Use `.claudeignore`. It matches `.cursorignore` and excludes generated, vendored, cache, Playwright, runtime upload, and large data paths such as `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, `dist/`, `.cache/`, `images/`, `thumbs/`, and `data/hts-rates.json`.

## Allowed Operations

- Allowed: read/edit repo files, run `npm` scripts, use `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push origin sandbox-albert`
- Not allowed by default: force-push, rewrite history, edit `node_modules/`
- SSH is not the normal deployment path. Deployment is Cloud Build -> Docker -> Cloud Run after branch push; use `gcloud` only for explicit rollback or incident triage.

## Tests

```powershell
npm test
npm test -- --testPathPattern="<name>"
```

Run the relevant tests before committing. `start:sandbox-albert` does not run tests before starting `nodemon`.

## Claude-Specific Rules

- For DB columns: model field + `models/db.js` inline migration + `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` assertion.
- Quote mixed-case Postgres column identifiers in inline migrations.
- Add code to `routes/lib.router.js` and `models/lib.model.js` surgically; they are large legacy files.
- Keep secrets out of `--set-env-vars`; use Secret Manager via `--set-secrets`.

## Commit Style

```text
type(scope): short description
```

Use `fix`, `feat`, `refactor`, `docs`, or `chore`. Keep messages specific.

If those two diverge, you get exactly your symptom — `res` shows counts but the sub-grid is empty. Let me check how the frontend actually loads detail rows and whether it still matches this new backend.

content.text:
The mechanism is now clear. The new backend uses **lazy vendor loading**: the list endpoint no longer embeds each item's `Vendors` (it returns `Res` from a separate count, and `Vendors: []`), expecting the frontend to fetch them per-row via `getRfqItemVendors`. So:

- A frontend that **has** the lazy loader → sub-grids work.
- A frontend that **doesn't** (older build, reads `params.data.Vendors`) → sub-grids are empty, but `Res` still shows.

"New design not there + empty sub-grids" strongly suggests the **deployed frontend is older than the deployed backend** — i.e., my backend `develop→sandbox-albert` merge deployed the new lazy backend, but the matching frontend didn't deploy. Let me verify what's actually live and when it deployed.

content.text:
Major finding — this flips the diagnosis. The **deployed** `rfq.module-A5RJCKWT.js` **already contains my changes and the matching lazy loader**:
- `getRfqItemVendors` / `loadRfqItemVendors`: present ✓ (compatible with the new backend)
- `rfq:density` (my density toggle): present ✓
- `buildRfqScenarioColumnGroups` / "General · FOB" (my groups): present ✓

So **the server is serving the new, matching frontend** — the design *is* deployed, and the frontend *can* lazy-load vendors. That means what you're seeing (old look + empty sub-grids) is almost certainly your **browser showing a cached old version** of the app, which still reads vendors the old way and chokes on the new backend. Let me check whether the app uses a service worker (which would cache the old app aggressively).

input.command:

echo "=== index.html cache headers + ngsw refs ==="
curl -s -I "https://alsand.designflow.app/" --max-time 12 | grep -iE "cache-control|etag|last-modified"
grep -oiE "ngsw|serviceworker|service-worker" alsand-index.html | sort -u

content.text:
That's the answer. Two concrete facts together explain everything:

1. **The new version is fully deployed and consistent on the server** — the live frontend already has my design *and* the matching new vendor-loading code, and the backend has its half. Server-side, nothing is broken or mismatched.
2. **The site serves its entry page (`index.html`) with a 30-day cache** (`max-age=2592000`). So any browser that opened the site *before* today's update keeps running the **old app from its cache** — and that old app reads factory quotes the old way, which the new backend no longer provides. Result: old look **and** empty sub-grids, exactly what you saw.

So nothing is actually lost or corrupted — your browser is just showing a stale copy.

## The immediate fix (you can do right now)
On the RFQ page, do a **hard refresh** to throw away the cached old app:
- **Windows:** `Ctrl` + `Shift` + `R`  (or `Ctrl` + `F5`)
- If that doesn't do it: open the browser menu → History/Privacy → **Clear cached images and files**, then reload.

After that you should see the new design **and** the factory quotes back in the sub-grids. Please try it and tell me if it comes back.

## Why this happened (plain version)
The performance update that came in from the team's main branch changed *how* factory quotes are delivered (loaded on-demand instead of all upfront). That's fine when the browser is running the matching new app — but a browser stuck on a cached old app can't keep up, so the quotes show blank. A refresh lines them back up.

## Worth fixing properly (so it stops recurring)
The real culprit is that `index.html` is cached for 30 days. Entry pages should be set to "always revalidate" so app updates reach everyone immediately without a manual hard-refresh. That's a small server config change on the frontend. I can look into it if you want — but first, try the hard refresh and let me know if your quotes reappear.

content.text:
While that compiles (~15s), here's the honest picture:

**Issue 1 — a real bug I introduced (fixable).** The collapsible price-group feature adds a *second* header row (the "General · FOB" band over the price columns). The grid's header area wasn't given enough height for that extra row, so its **top half gets clipped** — which is the cut-off header you're seeing. Worse, the clipped band is where the expand/collapse controls live, so the groups look like they're not even there. This is my bug and I'll fix it.

**Issue 2 — an expectations gap I should have flagged louder.** The colorful "Claude Design" screenshots are a **full visual mockup** of a redesigned grid. The task I was given (the handoff README) explicitly said those mockups are *references*, and to **recreate four specific behaviors inside the existing grid** — not to repaint the whole page. So even working perfectly, the result is "your current grid **plus** four targeted features," which is *meant* to look ~90% like today's site. It was never going to look 99% like the mockup unless we do a much bigger visual reskin. That's a real choice we should make deliberately, not something I can hide behind a bug fix.

Also: the **frozen Picture/Description columns don't show because you earlier chose "respect saved layouts"** — your saved View keeps the old column setup. That's another reason it looks unchanged.

Let me wait for the server, then I'll fix the header clipping and show you. First, let me confirm it's up.

  if grep -qiE "error TS|failed to compile" /tmp/ng-fix.log 2>/dev/null; then echo "COMPILE ERROR"; tail -5 /tmp/ng-fix.log; break; fi
  sleep 8
done

content.text: Let me measure the header geometry directly to confirm and quantify the clipping.
Let me probe the exact header layout and experiment with header-height options live (I can set them via the grid API without recompiling, find values that fix the clip, then bake them in).

lastPrompt: it was partly implemented because i see options for compact and comfortable views. and the tope half of the header row is cut off. but look at the screenshots Claude Design gave. none of those changes…

content.text: The root cause is now unambiguous. The header has **three** rows (group band, column headers, filter), and the old CSS hack `::ng-deep .ag-header-row { height: 60px !important }` forces **every** row to 60px — but the grid positions them only **32px apart** (group 307, column 339, filter 371). So each row's bottom 28px is **overlapped/hidden by the next row** — that's your "top half cut off." My density change (setting a 32px header height) + the new group row exposed this old fixed-pixel hack. Let me verify the fix live by making the row heights match their slots.

The fix is proven live — setting `groupHeaderHeight`/`headerHeight`/`floatingFiltersHeight` as explicit options and making the row-height CSS match gives a clean, non-overlapping header (rows now stack at 30 / 58 / 40 with no overlap, container 129px). Let me capture it so you can see, then bake it into the code.

lastPrompt: it was partly implemented because i see options for compact and comfortable views. and the tope half of the header row is cut off. but look at the screenshots Claude Design gave. none of those changes…

content.text:
Fixed, pushed, and cleaned up (working tree is clean; local server stopped).

## What I just fixed
The cut-off header was a real bug from the collapsible price-groups feature: the group band added a third header row, and an old styling rule made the rows overlap, hiding the band (and the expand/collapse controls). I corrected the header heights and **verified it live against your sandbox data** — the three header rows now stack cleanly with no overlap and "General · FOB" shows in full. Committed as `bf0e4820` and pushed, so the sandbox is **rebuilding now (~5 minutes)**.

When it's done, do the **hard refresh** again (`Ctrl`+`Shift`+`R`) and the band/quote-groups will render properly.

## But I need to be straight with you about the bigger thing
Even after this fix and a refresh, the page will **still look mostly like your current site** — because that's what the work actually was. The colorful "Claude Design" mockups are a *full redesign*; the task I was handed (the handoff doc) explicitly said to treat those as **references** and add **four specific features** into the existing grid, not to repaint it. So what's there now is: your normal grid **+** density toggle, collapsible price columns, a frozen-column divider, and the gapless quote panel. It was never going to look 99% like the mockup.

That's a real fork, and it's your call which way to go:

options.label: Go for the full redesign look
options.description: Pursue the colorful mockup look (pink theme, restyled rows/headers, full visual overhaul). Much larger effort beyond the original handoff scope; I'd scope it as a separate piece of work.

content.text:
The themePink in the repo is an unused experiment with non-brand colors, and the RFQ grid runs on the **legacy `ag-theme-alpine`** CSS theme (with lots of existing overrides + the fixes we just made). Swapping to AG Grid's new theme engine would break all of that and risk the calculations. So the safe, correct way to get the mockup look is to **restyle the existing grid with CSS using the brand tokens** — closely approximating the design without touching the grid engine.

## Redesign plan (staged — I'll show each in the browser before moving on)
1. **Grid look & palette** — header style, row striping, pink selected/hover row, lighter borders, system font, rounded corners, right-aligned numbers. *(biggest visual shift)*
2. **Toolbar & buttons** — pink primary "Add Product", quiet outline secondaries, grouped controls with dividers.
3. **Pills & color-coded cells** — Status/Step as colored pills; scenario price columns tinted blue (General) / violet (Licensed); re-quote orange.
4. **Factory-quote panel** — card styling to match the mockup.

Each stage is one commit, verified live against your sandbox data with a screenshot.

**Realistic expectation:** this approximates the mockup closely; it won't be pixel-identical, since I'm theming the real grid rather than rebuilding it — that keeps your pricing math and the fixes safe.

Meanwhile, the **header fix is deploying** (~5 min) — once it's live, hard-refresh and the cut-off band will be gone regardless of the redesign.

Let me start **Stage 1** now and apply the core theme.

content.text: Stage 1 did **not** take effect — the theme variables aren't on the grid element. Let me find out why (what class the grid root actually has, and whether my CSS made it into the page).

Aha — crucial discovery. The grid is **not** on the legacy `ag-theme-alpine` CSS theme; its real classes are Theming-API-generated (`ag-theme-params-1`, `ag-theme-buttonStyle-1`, …). So my `.ag-theme-alpine` variable overrides matched nothing. The repo uses AG Grid's **Theming API**, which means recoloring must go through a theme object (`withParams`), not legacy CSS vars. Let me find where the theme is wired.

content.text: The theme runs through AG Grid's **Theming API** (default `themeQuartz`), set by a shared service — so legacy CSS-variable overrides on `.ag-theme-alpine` are inert. The Theming API does honor `--ag-*` overrides when set on the **actual grid element**, though. Let me test that escape hatch live before committing to an approach.

content.text: Neither CSS path works — the Theming API generates high-specificity styles from a **theme object**, so the only correct way to recolor is through `themeQuartz.withParams(...)`. Good to know before committing anything. Let me redo Stage 1 the right way: define a proper brand theme and apply it to the RFQ grid. First, replace my dead CSS with correct-selector rules:

Done with this checkpoint. Here's where things stand.

## Shipped this round (both deploying now, ~5 min)
1. **Header clipping bug — fixed** (`bf0e4820`). The cut-off band is gone; the three header rows (group · column · filter) now stack cleanly. Verified live.
2. **Redesign Stage 1 — brand theme** (`86d641a2`). Applied the Designflow palette to the RFQ grid: pink accent, ink text, soft header (`#f7f9fc`), subtle zebra striping, brand-soft selected row, lighter borders, rounded corners, the system font, and uppercase/tabular headers. Verified live — and it preserved the row height, frozen seam, scenario tints, and header fix.

**To see both:** wait ~5 min, then hard-refresh (`Ctrl`+`Shift`+`R`).

## Important things to know
- **A key technical discovery:** your grid uses AG Grid's modern *Theming API* (not plain CSS), so the redesign has to go through theme objects. My first attempt used the old CSS approach and did nothing — I caught it via live inspection and redid it correctly. That's why this is now on solid footing for the remaining stages.
- **Set expectations:** Stage 1 is the *foundation* — colors, typography, cleaner chrome. It will look noticeably tidier and on-brand, but the **bigger visual shifts come in stages 2–3** (pink primary button + quiet secondaries, colored Status/Step pills, blue/violet tinted scenario price cells). It still won't be pixel-identical to the mockup, but it'll move clearly toward it.
- **Heads-up — you have another session editing this same project right now** (an "Item Detail redesign" — new fonts in `index.html`, `itemDetail` component changes). I left those files completely untouched, but two sessions in one repo can collide. Worth being aware of.

## Next
Stages remaining: **2)** toolbar & buttons, **3)** pills & colored scenario cells, **4)** factory-quote panel card. Want me to continue straight through 2–4 (I'll verify each live and push), or pause so you can hard-refresh and react to Stage 1 first?

content.text:
Here's a ready-to-paste prompt for Claude Design. It explains where the previous handoff steered the implementation wrong, gives the real technical constraints of the live app, and asks for the design back in a form that can actually reproduce the renderings.

---

**Prompt for Claude Design — RFQ redesign, corrected handoff**

You produced a handoff package ("design_handoff_rfq_polish") plus high-fidelity renderings (RFQ Prototype.html / RFQ Redesign.html) for the Designflow RFQ page. It was implemented in the real Angular + AG Grid app, but the result does **not** look like your renderings. I want it to look like the renderings. Before you redo the spec, here's what was wrong with the last handoff and the real environment you must design within. Please read all of it, then produce a corrected, environment-aware design spec (format requested at the bottom).

**1. The scope framing was wrong.**
Your README explicitly said the HTML/JSX are "design references… not production code," that the task was to "recreate four specific behaviors in the existing grid," and that the result is "*meant* to look ~90% like today's site." So it was built as four targeted features bolted onto the existing grid — exactly as written. But what I actually want is the **full visual transformation in the renderings** (the pink-branded, restyled grid). The next handoff must be an explicit, full **visual spec of every surface**, not a behavior list, and it must say so up front.

**2. Your CSS snippets don't recolor this grid — it uses AG Grid's Theming API, not CSS themes.**
This is the single biggest miss. The grid is **not** on a legacy `ag-theme-alpine` CSS theme. It runs on AG Grid v35's **Theming API** (`themeQuartz` applied via a shared theme service), which emits machine-generated classes (`ag-theme-params-1`, `ag-theme-buttonStyle-1`, …) and ignores `.ag-theme-alpine` and `--ag-*` CSS-variable overrides. Concretely:
- Your snippets like `.ag-row-selected { background }`, `.ag-pinned-left-header { border-right }`, and `--ag-*` variable overrides **do not change colors** here.
- All palette/typography must be expressed as **theme parameters** passed to `themeQuartz.withParams({...})`, or as **theme parts**. Structural CSS (heights, borders, seams) can still be done with `::ng-deep` on *real* AG classes (`.ag-header-group-cell`, `.ag-cell-last-left-pinned`, etc.), but **color/typography cannot.**
- So design against the Theming API knobs. The ones available include (use these names): `accentColor, backgroundColor, foregroundColor, textColor, fontFamily, fontSize, headerBackgroundColor, headerTextColor, headerFontWeight, headerFontSize, headerColumnBorder, borderColor, borderRadius, wrapperBorder, wrapperBorderRadius, rowBorder, rowVerticalPaddingScale, cellHorizontalPadding, spacing, oddRowBackgroundColor, rowHoverColor, selectedRowBackgroundColor, rangeSelectionBackgroundColor, rangeSelectionBorderColor, columnBorder`, plus parts (`iconSet`, `colorScheme`, `checkboxStyle`, `inputStyle`, `tabStyle`).
- **Anything in your renderings that isn't expressible as a theme param** (status/step pills, the colored scenario cells, the pink primary button, the factory-quote "card" panel, the barbell toolbar) **requires a custom Angular cell/header renderer or component-scoped CSS** — it cannot come from the theme. For each such element, say explicitly "this needs a cell renderer" and provide the exact markup/styles.

**3. "Pin/size by default" via column definitions does not stick.**
Column pinned-state, width, and order are restored from the user's saved **Views** (backend `GridLayout` / column-state), which override colDef defaults on load. So "freeze Picture/Description by default" silently does nothing for anyone with a saved view. If the design needs a layout default, the spec must say whether it should (a) be forced on every load (overriding saved views) or (b) ship as an updated default View. Don't assume a colDef default is enough.

**4. The grid has structure your prototype omits — design around it, not over it.**
The real grid has: a **group-band header row** (collapsible Gen/Lic × FOB/POE/WHSE/MDDP scenario groups) **on top of** the column-header row **on top of** a **floating-filter row** (three stacked header rows — your renderings show one); **row grouping** by RFQ Group (gray group rows); **master/detail** expansion (the factory-quote sub-grid); a **status bar**, **sidebar/columns panel**, **fill handle**, and **role-based column hiding** (non-admin users see a subset — e.g. only "Price for Sales", not Sell/Margin). The spec must show the header in its real 3-row form and style the group band, and must define states for grouped rows, the filter row, and the role-reduced column set.

**5. The factory-quote sub-grid is backend-driven and lazy-loaded.**
Its columns come from a backend layout (live colIds are `price`/`name`/`note`, not the config's `quote_price`/`vendor_note`), and rows load on row-expand. Style it as the detail renderer it is; don't assume it shares the master grid's column ids.

**6. Typography / consistency.**
The master grid currently uses a system font stack. A parallel "Item Detail redesign" is introducing **Hanken Grotesk** (UI) + **IBM Plex Mono** (codes/figures). Decide and state the intended app-wide type system so RFQ matches Item Detail rather than diverging.

**What I need from you (deliverables):**
1. A one-line scope statement: "full visual reskin of the RFQ grid," not a behavior list.
2. A **`themeQuartz.withParams({...})` parameter table** — every value, using the exact param names above — that reproduces the rendering's grid chrome (background, header, rows, zebra, borders, radius, accent, selection, spacing, fonts).
3. A **per-element spec for everything the theme can't do**, each tagged `[theme param]`, `[::ng-deep CSS on <class>]`, or `[needs cell/header renderer]`: Status pill, Step pill, scenario price cells (blue General / violet Licensed body tints + the always-visible "Price for Sales" emphasis), re-quote orange cell, the frozen identity seam, the pink primary "Add Product" button + quiet secondaries, toolbar grouping/dividers, status-bar styling, and the factory-quote panel card (header, table, chips, barbell action bar).
4. The **header in its true 3-row form** (group band · column · floating filter) with heights and the group-band tint per family.
5. Exact **tokens** (hex, radius, font weights, sizes) as before — they were good; keep them — but mapped to the mechanisms above.
6. A short **"feasible vs. needs-engineering"** note: which parts are pure theming (cheap) vs. which require renderers/components (so we can sequence the work).

Assume AG Grid Enterprise v35 (Theming API), Angular 21, module-based (non-standalone) components. Don't propose replacing the grid or its calculation/edit behavior — only its appearance.

---

Want me to also fold in the exact hex tokens from your original handoff so the prompt is fully self-contained (in case Claude Design doesn't still have them)?

questions.question: At the very start I merged develop into sandbox-albert on the BACKEND repo (your normal pre-work sync). That merge brought the 'RFQ performance' change whose lazy quote-loading, combined with stale browser cache, caused the empty sub-grids. Undo that backend merge too?

options.label: Keep the backend merge
options.description: Leave sandbox-albert synced with develop (your standard workflow). The empty sub-grids were a browser-cache issue and resolve with a hard refresh. Recommended — reverting it rolls back the whole team's develop sync on this branch.

options.label: Undo the backend merge
options.description: Revert the develop->sandbox-albert merge on the backend, restoring the older quote-loading. Broader change: removes all of develop's updates from sandbox-albert, not just the RFQ part, and redeploys the old backend.

## Everything I did is undone

| Repo | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Frontend** | Reverted my 6 RFQ commits (`3b090fc8`…`86d641a2`) → `268e04ef` | RFQ page back to its pre-session look/behavior. 77 tests pass. |

Both branches will **redeploy automatically (~5 min each)**. After they finish, do one **hard refresh** (`Ctrl`+`Shift`+`R`) and you'll have the original RFQ page back, with factory quotes showing in the sub-grids again (the backend revert restores the eager loading that the old frontend expects).

**Two things I preserved (not mine, left untouched):**

- A backend **docs** commit (`da9152a`).

To bring develop's updates back into sandbox-albert in the future, that revert will itself need to be reverted (or the team re-merges deliberately). Worth flagging to whoever manages the branch so it doesn't cause confusion.

content.text:
Good question, and it's the right one to ask. Honest rundown — a few real downsides, balanced against the fact that syncing is normal and healthy long-term:

**The downsides specific to right now:**

1. **The empty-sub-grid problem could come back.** The team's "performance" change is exactly what triggered it: it changed how factory quotes load, and that only works smoothly if your browser is running the matching new version. Your site caches the app aggressively (up to 30 days), so anyone whose browser is still showing an old cached copy would see empty quotes again — until they hard-refresh. So syncing now, without first fixing that caching setting, risks re-running the same incident for you and other users.

2. **It brings *everything* the team has changed, not just the one thing.** "Latest team changes" is a bundle — other features and fixes you haven't seen ride along. Most are probably fine, but you'd be accepting all of it at once, sight unseen.

3. **It could collide with the Item Detail redesign that's in progress.** Someone's actively working on another part of the app on this same branch right now. Pulling in a big batch of changes mid-flight can create conflicts that need untangling.

4. **The mechanical hiccup I mentioned** — but that one's mine to handle, not a real risk to you.

**The flip side (why you'd eventually want to):** staying separate from the team forever isn't healthy — your branch drifts further from everyone else's, and the longer you wait, the bigger and messier the eventual catch-up. Syncing regularly is normal practice; you just don't want to do it blindly the same day something broke.

**My recommendation:** don't sync today. The cleaner sequence is — first fix the root cause (that 30-day cache so app updates reach everyone immediately and stale-browser breakage stops happening), *then* pulling in team changes becomes low-risk and routine. When you're ready, just say so and I'll do the sync safely and check the sub-grids actually work before you rely on it.

Want me to look into fixing that caching issue? That's the thing that would make all of this safer.

Found it. In `nginx.conf`, lines 84–87 apply a **30-day cache to `.js`, `.css`, AND `.html`** — including `index.html`. That's the root cause: the entry page (which points to the current app build) gets cached for 30 days, so browsers keep loading an old app after a deploy. The JS/CSS are content-hashed (safe to cache long), but **`index.html` must never be long-cached**.

file.content:
# ==========================
# Stage 1: Build Angular App
# ==========================
FROM node:lts AS builder

# Set build-time environment variables
ARG NODE_ENV
ENV NODE_ENV=${NODE_ENV}
ARG COMMIT_SHA=local

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Activate Yarn 4 (Berry) — lockfile is in v4 format; node:lts ships corepack
RUN corepack enable && corepack prepare yarn@4.13.0 --activate

# Install dependencies (leverage caching for faster builds)
COPY package.json yarn.lock .yarnrc.yml ./
RUN yarn install --immutable

# Copy source code, run tests, then build Angular app
COPY . .
RUN TS=$(node -e " \
      const d=new Date(); \
      const date=d.toLocaleDateString('en-CA',{timeZone:'America/New_York'}); \
      const time=d.toLocaleTimeString('en-US',{timeZone:'America/New_York',hour:'numeric',minute:'2-digit',hour12:true,timeZoneName:'short'}); \
      console.log(date+' '+time);") && \
    printf 'export const APP_VERSION = {\n  commitHash: '"'"'%s'"'"',\n  pushedAt: '"'"'%s'"'"',\n};\n' \
    "$(echo "${COMMIT_SHA}" | cut -c1-7)" "$TS" > src/app/version.ts
RUN yarn test
RUN yarn build:${NODE_ENV}


# ===============================
# Stage 2: Serve App with NGINX
# ===============================
FROM macbre/nginx-brotli:1.19.10

# Copy custom NGINX configuration
COPY --from=builder /app/nginx.conf /etc/nginx/nginx.conf

# Copy built Angular app to NGINX html directory
COPY --from=builder /app/dist/browser /usr/share/nginx/html

# Expose HTTP port
EXPOSE 80

# Default command provided by the base NGINX image

Here's the surgical fix: carve `index.html` out into its own block with `no-cache`, drop `html` from the 30-day bucket, and re-state the security headers (an `add_header` in a location replaces the inherited ones).

+        # the server on every load (cheap 304s), so new deploys reach everyone
+        # immediately. The JS/CSS it references are content-hashed and long-cached
+        # below. Security headers are restated here because an add_header inside a
+        # Content-hashed build assets (filename changes every build) — safe to cache
+        # long. 'html' is intentionally NOT here; index.html is handled above.

content.text: Config is valid (braces balanced 8/8, standard nginx; no local nginx/docker to run `nginx -t`, but a bad config would just fail the Cloud Run rollout and keep the old revision serving — it won't take the site down). Committing and pushing the fix:

input.command:
cd /d/repos/dflow/designflow-frontend
git add nginx.conf && git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(nginx): never long-cache index.html so deploys reach users immediately

The catch-all `location ~* \.(js|css|pdf|html|swf)$ { expires 30d }` block was
giving index.html a 30-day cache. Because index.html is the SPA entry point that
references the current build's hashed JS/CSS, browsers kept running a stale app
for up to 30 days after a deploy — and broke (e.g. empty RFQ sub-grids) when the
backend moved on without them.

- Add an exact-match `location = /index.html` with `Cache-Control: no-cache`
  (browser may store but must revalidate every load → cheap 304s, instant
  pickup of new builds). Security headers restated there since an add_header in
  a location replaces the inherited http-level ones.
- Drop `html` from the 30-day bucket. Hashed JS/CSS stay long-cached (their
  filenames change per build, so that's safe).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin sandbox-albert 2>&1 | tail -3 && git log --oneline -2
input.description: Commit and push the nginx cache fix

content.text:
Done — pushed as `7e9ca2d7`, deploying with the next build (~5 min).

## What this fixes, in plain terms
Your site was telling browsers "keep this page for 30 days." Since that page is what loads the whole app, people kept running an old version for weeks after an update — and that's what made things break when the backend changed underneath them. Now the entry page is set to **"always check for the latest version,"** while the heavy files (which are safe to keep) stay cached. So from now on, **app updates reach everyone the moment they reload — no more hard-refreshes needed.**

## One catch (just once more)
Your browser is *still holding* the old "keep for 30 days" instruction from before this fix. So there's **one final hard-refresh** needed (`Ctrl`+`Shift`+`R`) after this deploys — for you and anyone currently stuck. After that single refresh, it's automatic forever; nobody has to think about it again.

## Scope
This fixes **your sandbox site** now. The same fix will reach the main/production sites later when your branch flows into the team's through the normal review process (it's the same config file everywhere) — so the whole app benefits eventually, not just RFQ.

## Why this mattered for your earlier question
This is the root-cause fix that makes **"pulling in the latest team changes" safe**. With the cache issue gone, a future sync won't strand people on a broken old version — which was the main downside I flagged. So when you're ready to pull in the team's updates, this groundwork is already done.

Nothing else needed from you right now except that one hard-refresh once it's live.

content:
# AI Session Documentation Update Prompt

Use this at the end of an AI coding session when the session changed code, deployment behavior, data flow, configuration, operational assumptions, or important project knowledge.

Your job is not to rebuild the entire documentation system. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...
```

1	# HANDOFF — Remaining open items
2	
3	Open items that need continuation context are listed below.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Factory bulk offer upload — **IMPLEMENTED, NOT YET COMMITTED** (updated 2026-06-11)
8	
9	A vendor-only "Upload Offers" feature was fully implemented across both `designflow-frontend` and `designflow-backend` in this session. All code sits as unstaged changes on branch `sandbox-albert` in both repos.
10	
11	### What it does
12	
13	Factory/vendor users see an **Upload Offers** button (`@if (role === 'vendor')`) in the RFQ toolbar. Clicking it opens a 3-stage dialog:
14	
15	1. **Form** — download a pre-formatted `.xlsx` template (pre-filled with factory name, date, Customer dropdown, Price Terms dropdown, 50 tall data rows with yellow image cells); name the RFQ group; drop-zone to select the completed file
16	2. **Uploading** — spinner while the API processes the file
17	3. **Result** — list of created items (price code + description) and any row-level errors (bilingual English · Chinese)
18	
19	On the backend, the import creates:
20	- One `RFQGroup` (named by the factory)
21	- One `RFQItem` per non-empty data row — price code `foff-XXXX` (global sequential; padded to 4 digits)
22	- One `RFQVendor` quote record per row (linked to the uploading factory's `factory_id` from JWT; `req_status = 2` = quoted; lands at @factories step)
23	- Embedded product images extracted from the xlsx and uploaded to DigitalOcean Spaces via `uploadBufferToSpaces`
24	
25	### Files changed — frontend
26	
27	| File | Change |
28	|---|---|
29	| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.ts` | **NEW** — `standalone: false`, `stage: 'form'|'uploading'|'result'`, JWT vendor name pre-fill, drag-and-drop file zone, `firstValueFrom()` calls |
30	| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.html` | **NEW** — 3-stage template |

# HANDOFF — Remaining open items

Open items that need continuation context are listed below.

---

## Factory bulk offer upload — **IMPLEMENTED, NOT YET COMMITTED** (updated 2026-06-11)

A vendor-only "Upload Offers" feature was fully implemented across both `designflow-frontend` and `designflow-backend` in this session. All code sits as unstaged changes on branch `sandbox-albert` in both repos.

### What it does

Factory/vendor users see an **Upload Offers** button (`@if (role === 'vendor')`) in the RFQ toolbar. Clicking it opens a 3-stage dialog:

1. **Form** — download a pre-formatted `.xlsx` template (pre-filled with factory name, date, Customer dropdown, Price Terms dropdown, 50 tall data rows with yellow image cells); name the RFQ group; drop-zone to select the completed file
2. **Uploading** — spinner while the API processes the file
3. **Result** — list of created items (price code + description) and any row-level errors (bilingual English · Chinese)

On the backend, the import creates:
- One `RFQGroup` (named by the factory)
- One `RFQItem` per non-empty data row — price code `foff-XXXX` (global sequential; padded to 4 digits)
- One `RFQVendor` quote record per row (linked to the uploading factory's `factory_id` from JWT; `req_status = 2` = quoted; lands at @factories step)
- Embedded product images extracted from the xlsx and uploaded to DigitalOcean Spaces via `uploadBufferToSpaces`

### Files changed — frontend

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.ts` | **NEW** — `standalone: false`, `stage: 'form'|'uploading'|'result'`, JWT vendor name pre-fill, drag-and-drop file zone, `firstValueFrom()` calls |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.html` | **NEW** — 3-stage template |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.scss` | **NEW** — drop zone, result, error banner styles |
| `src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts` | Added `getRFQFactoryTemplate()` (GET → blob) and `importFactoryRFQ(formData)` (POST → multipart) |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` | Added `onFactoryBulkUpload()` — decodes JWT for vendor name, opens dialog, refreshes grid + snackbar on success |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.html` | Added `@if (role === 'vendor')` Upload Offers button in toolbar |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.module.ts` | Added `FactoryBulkUploadDialogComponent` to `declarations` |

### Files changed — backend (see `designflow-backend/HANDOFF.md`)

`helpers/rfqBulkImport.js` (new), `helpers/digitalocean.js`, `helpers/multer.js`, `models/lib.model.js`, `controllers/main.controller.js`, `routes/lib.router.js`.

### Current state

- All changes are on `sandbox-albert`, **unstaged** (no `git add`, no `git commit`)
- `develop` was merged into `sandbox-albert` again on 2026-06-11 across the child repos; local uncommitted frontend/backend work was preserved.
- 2026-06-11 UX hardening added bilingual disabled-state/error feedback around this workflow: `Import Offers` now explains missing offer group name and/or `.xlsx` file; download/upload/file-type errors are user-visible in English and Simplified Mandarin.
- Backend copy guard messaging was also clarified to name missing/inactive Product Type / Sub-Type / Sub-Sub-Type instead of only the old pre-May-2025 wording.
- Backend: `npm test -- --runInBand rfqCopyMerchGroupGuard` passed on 2026-06-11.
- Frontend focused tests passed on 2026-06-11 via `npm test -- --testPathPatterns=addNewProduct.component --runInBand`, `addNewProductGrid`, `duplicate_dialog`, `rfq.component`, and `rfq-status-validation`.
- There is no `rfq-button` spec; vendor decline / Keep Price snackbar changes were not covered by a dedicated test.
- **Per push policy: do not push until Albert says so**

### Next action

git add src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/ \

over description fields in `data/hts-rates.json`. Inject the top 5 matches into the AI's

**Why it matters:** The system prompt covers 9 chapters. For products outside those chapters,

## Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration — Phase-0 bridge (updated 2026-06-14)

**Status:** shared-key verification **DONE (GO, 99.3%)**; Phase-0 bridge **designed, not yet built**.

**Context:** See `docs/licensing-tracking-poppim-integration.md` (analysis + verified result) and
`docs/licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md` (the build spec). Albert's direction (2026-06-13): DesignFlow
stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim (Directus app at `pm.designflow.app`) consumes a
**read-only** copy.

**Done (this session):**
- Confirmed both data models; the two run on separate clouds/DBs (API↔API, no shared DB).
- **Verified the join key (GO):** DesignFlow `item_num_id` ⇄ the item-number prefix of poppim
  `product.name` (NOT `product.code`). **9,224 / 9,293 (99.3%)** poppim item-nos match DesignFlow; misses
  are truncated codes. Matches are almost entirely licensed (div 1/2/8). Key is **not** unique on
  `item_num_id` alone → use `(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`.
- Wrote the Phase-0 bridge spec (read-only, one-way DesignFlow→poppim status mirror).

**Next action (build the bridge — see `docs/licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md`):**

   `SELECT id, phase, status, "order" FROM designflow."LicenseFeedBacks" ORDER BY "order";`; obtain a
   Directus service-account write token; pick hosting (Cloud Run job vs Directus Flow).

   schema repo.

**Risks / watchouts:**

content.content:
522	## 13. Deployment
523	
524	See `docs/deployment.md` for the full pipeline. Summary:
525	
526	- **CI/CD:** Google Cloud Build (`cloudbuild.yaml`) — no GitHub Actions
527	- **Build:** Multi-stage Docker (`Dockerfile`) — Node LTS builds Angular, nginx-brotli serves it
528	- **Registry:** Google Artifact Registry (`{REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/...`)
529	- **Runtime:** Google Cloud Run (managed, serverless)
530	- **Deploy trigger:** Cloud Build trigger on push to the relevant branch
531	- **Rollback:** Cloud Run revision rollback via `gcloud run services update-traffic`
532	- **Environment config:** Compiled into the Angular bundle at build time (no runtime env injection)
533	
534	---
535	
536	## 14. Critical incident log
537	
538	### 2025 — RFQ calculations silently broken by `aggFunc` on Date Modified
539	
540	**What happened:** Adding `aggFunc: 'max'` to the Date Modified column (commit `43db88d`) activated AG Grid Enterprise's full aggregation pipeline. All group rows lost `params.data` (became `undefined`) in every valueSetter and valueGetter.
541	
542	**Impact:** Landed cost, freight, and all 16 margin calculations silently returned wrong values or threw for any group row. Users saw blank cells.
543	
544	**Root cause:** AG Grid Enterprise activates aggregation for the entire grid (not just the column) when any column has an `aggFunc`. Group rows then use aggregated values, not `params.data`.
545	
546	**Recovery:** Removed `aggFunc` entirely from `applyAutoFormatters()`. Group ordering is handled by `postSortRows` + `comparator` without needing aggregation (`0a2f4f7`).
547	
548	**Rule added:** Never add `aggFunc` to a column in the RFQ grid unless you have confirmed that all valueSetters and valueGetters gracefully handle `params.data === undefined` for group rows.
549	
550	---
551	
552	### 2025 — CBM/pc cell edit always reverted on save (first root cause)
553	
554	**What happened:** Any value typed into CBM/pc (or any column calling `recalculate_Lic_Gen`) disappeared after pressing Enter. The AG Grid cell reverted to empty.
555	
556	**Root cause (primary):** All 16 margin setter functions in `equation.ts` accessed `params.column.userProvidedColDef.colId`. In AG Grid v35, `userProvidedColDef` is `undefined`, so every call threw a `TypeError`. Because the valueSetter is `async`, AG Grid v35 awaited the rejected Promise and cancelled the edit.
557	
558	**Root cause (secondary):** `duty_rate.title` hard access (without optional chaining) threw if `duty_rate` was null.
559	
560	**Recovery:** Replaced all `userProvidedColDef` access with `getColDef?.()` (`daf2bf9`); guarded all `duty_rate?.title` accesses (`b77d3b0`).
561

file.content:
## 13. Deployment

See `docs/deployment.md` for the full pipeline. Summary:

- **CI/CD:** Google Cloud Build (`cloudbuild.yaml`) — no GitHub Actions
- **Build:** Multi-stage Docker (`Dockerfile`) — Node LTS builds Angular, nginx-brotli serves it
- **Registry:** Google Artifact Registry (`{REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/...`)
- **Runtime:** Google Cloud Run (managed, serverless)
- **Deploy trigger:** Cloud Build trigger on push to the relevant branch
- **Rollback:** Cloud Run revision rollback via `gcloud run services update-traffic`
- **Environment config:** Compiled into the Angular bundle at build time (no runtime env injection)

---

## 14. Critical incident log

### 2025 — RFQ calculations silently broken by `aggFunc` on Date Modified

**What happened:** Adding `aggFunc: 'max'` to the Date Modified column (commit `43db88d`) activated AG Grid Enterprise's full aggregation pipeline. All group rows lost `params.data` (became `undefined`) in every valueSetter and valueGetter.

**Impact:** Landed cost, freight, and all 16 margin calculations silently returned wrong values or threw for any group row. Users saw blank cells.

**Root cause:** AG Grid Enterprise activates aggregation for the entire grid (not just the column) when any column has an `aggFunc`. Group rows then use aggregated values, not `params.data`.

**Recovery:** Removed `aggFunc` entirely from `applyAutoFormatters()`. Group ordering is handled by `postSortRows` + `comparator` without needing aggregation (`0a2f4f7`).

**Rule added:** Never add `aggFunc` to a column in the RFQ grid unless you have confirmed that all valueSetters and valueGetters gracefully handle `params.data === undefined` for group rows.

---

### 2025 — CBM/pc cell edit always reverted on save (first root cause)

**What happened:** Any value typed into CBM/pc (or any column calling `recalculate_Lic_Gen`) disappeared after pressing Enter. The AG Grid cell reverted to empty.

**Root cause (primary):** All 16 margin setter functions in `equation.ts` accessed `params.column.userProvidedColDef.colId`. In AG Grid v35, `userProvidedColDef` is `undefined`, so every call threw a `TypeError`. Because the valueSetter is `async`, AG Grid v35 awaited the rejected Promise and cancelled the edit.

**Root cause (secondary):** `duty_rate.title` hard access (without optional chaining) threw if `duty_rate` was null.

**Recovery:** Replaced all `userProvidedColDef` access with `getColDef?.()` (`daf2bf9`); guarded all `duty_rate?.title` accesses (`b77d3b0`).

file.content:
**Why:** The first implementation (`e8ba0551`) set `sort`/`sortIndex`/`comparator` only on colId `quote_price`. At runtime that column does not exist (the live one is `price`), so the sort silently did nothing and the `Name` column kept owning the sort. Generalized to match either colId in `b241ce79`.

**Do not change because:** Hardcoding a single colId reintroduces the silent no-op — the sort lands on a column that isn't on screen and `Name` wins. Until the detail grid is migrated to the static `rfq.detail.grid.config.ts` (config-migration is noted as a TODO in `detail-cell-renderer.component.ts`), the backend colId is the source of truth and may be `price`. The bottom-sink rule depends on `req_status == 1` meaning "declined" (see §8).

---

## 12. Credentials and environment

The bottom-sink rule depends on `req_status == 1` meaning "declined" (see §8).

## 12. Credentials and environment
input.new_string:
**Do not change because:** Hardcoding a single colId reintroduces the silent no-op — the sort lands on a column that isn't on screen and `Name` wins. Until the detail grid is migrated to the static `rfq.detail.grid.config.ts` (config-migration is noted as a TODO in `detail-cell-renderer.component.ts`), the backend colId is the source of truth and may be `price`. The bottom-sink rule depends on `req_status == 1` meaning "declined" (see §8).

---

### The RFQ grid uses AG Grid's Theming API (`themeQuartz`), NOT the legacy CSS theme

**Looks like:** `rfq.component.html` sets `class="ag-theme-alpine"` and `angular.json` loads `ag-theme-alpine.css`, so it looks like a legacy CSS-themed grid you can restyle with `.ag-theme-alpine { --ag-*: ... }` overrides.

**Actually:** The grid runs on AG Grid v35's **Theming API**. `GridUISetupService.bootstrap()` (`src/@vex/services/grid-ui.service.ts`, called from `onGridReady`) does `gridApi.setGridOption('theme', <theme>)` from `GRID_THEME_CONFIG` (`src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts`, default `themeQuartz`). The rendered grid element carries machine-generated classes (`ag-theme-params-1`, `ag-theme-buttonStyle-1`, ...); the `ag-theme-alpine` class is inert and legacy `--ag-*` CSS-variable overrides do **not** recolor it.

**Why:** Verified 2026-06-15 — a redesign attempt overrode `.ag-theme-alpine` variables and computed styles showed zero effect; the live grid element had no `ag-theme-alpine` class.

**Future sessions should:** To restyle the grid's palette/typography, build a `Theme` with `themeQuartz.withParams({...})` and apply it (e.g. `setGridOption('theme', ...)` after `bootstrap()`, RFQ-scoped, so the shared theme service and other grids are unaffected). Structural CSS (row/header heights, borders, the frozen seam) still works via `::ng-deep` on **real** AG classes (`.ag-header-group-cell`, `.ag-cell-last-left-pinned`, `.ag-header-row-group/-column/-filter`). Pills, tinted cells, and custom buttons need cell/header renderers — they are not theme params.

---

### Saved Views (backend column-state) override colDef defaults on load

**Looks like:** Setting `pinned` / `width` / `hide` / order on a colDef in `rfq.grid.config.ts` should be the on-screen default.

**Actually:** On load, `applyGridViewStateForViewName()` calls `gridApi.applyColumnState({ state, applyOrder: true })` using the backend `GridLayout` view-state, which overrides colDef `pinned`/`width`/order. Verified 2026-06-15: a colDef change to pin Picture/Description (and new widths) did nothing live — `getColumnState()` showed the saved view's old `pinned: null` / widths winning.

**Future sessions should:** If a layout default must always apply, force it after the view restore (`applyColumnState` in `onGridReady` after the view loads) or update the saved Default view. A colDef default alone won't stick for users with a saved view.

---

## 12. Credentials and environment

content.text: Now the frontend §14 incident entry for the cache issue:

input.old_string:
## 14. Critical incident log

### 2025 — RFQ calculations silently broken by `aggFunc` on Date Modified
input.new_string:
## 14. Critical incident log

### 2026-06-15 — Stale `index.html` cache served an old frontend (empty RFQ sub-grids after deploy)

**What happened:** After a backend change to how RFQ factory quotes load (a `develop` "RFQ performance" refactor → lazy per-row vendor loading via a new `getRfqItemVendors` endpoint), users saw empty detail sub-grids even though the `Res` column showed quote counts, and the page looked unchanged after frontend deploys.

**Root cause:** `nginx.conf` served `index.html` with a 30-day cache (the catch-all `location ~* \.(js|css|pdf|html|swf)$ { expires 30d }` block included `html`). Browsers kept running a **cached old frontend** that loads quotes the old (eager) way; against the new lazy backend it rendered empty. The deployed app was actually correct and matched — the browser was stale.

**Recovery:** Fixed `nginx.conf` (`7e9ca2d7`): `index.html` is now `Cache-Control: no-cache` via an exact-match `location = /index.html` block (security headers restated there since an `add_header` in a location replaces inherited ones), and `html` was removed from the long-cache bucket; content-hashed JS/CSS stay long-cached. Currently-cached browsers need one hard refresh; afterward, deploys reach everyone immediately. The triggering backend merge was reverted on `sandbox-albert` (see `designflow-backend` §14). Detail: `docs/deployment.md → Static asset caching`.

**Rule added:** Never long-cache `index.html` (or any SPA entry HTML) — cache only content-hashed assets. Any rollout that changes a frontend↔backend data contract (e.g. lazy loading) will break browsers pinned to a stale entry page until the cache fix is deployed AND they reload.

---

### 2025 — RFQ calculations silently broken by `aggFunc` on Date Modified

### 2025 — RFQ calculations silently broken by `aggFunc` on Date Modified

**What happened:** Adding `aggFunc: 'max'` to the Date Modified column (commit `43db88d`) activated AG Grid Enterprise's full aggregation pipeline. All group rows lost `params.data` (became `undefined`) in every valueSetter and valueGetter.

content.content:
24	## Docker image
25	
26	**Build stages:**
27	
28	1. **Builder** (`node:lts`):
29	   - `WORKDIR /app`
30	   - `yarn install --frozen-lockfile --production=false`
31	   - `yarn test` — **tests must pass or the build fails**
32	   - `yarn build:${NODE_ENV}` — Angular build with environment substitution
33	   - Output: `dist/browser/`
34	
35	2. **Runtime** (`macbre/nginx-brotli:1.19.10`):
36	   - Copies `nginx.conf`
37	   - Copies `dist/browser/` to `/usr/share/nginx/html/`
38	   - Exposes port 80
39	   - Brotli-enabled nginx serves the SPA
40	
41	**Image tag format:**
42	```
43	{REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/{PROJECT_ID}/{ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/{APP_ENV}/{APP_NAME}:{BUILD_ID}-{COMMIT_SHA}
44	```
45	
46	Example:
47	```
48	us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/designflow/production/designflow-frontend:abc123-d4e5f6a7
49	```
50	
51	## Cloud Run service
52	
53	The image is deployed to Google Cloud Run (managed platform, not GKE). Cloud Run scales to zero when idle. Configuration:
54	
55	| Parameter | Controlled by | Default (from cloudbuild.yaml) |
56	|---|---|---|
57	| CPU | `_CPU` trigger variable | `1` |
58	| Memory | `_MEMORY` trigger variable | `512Mi` |
59	| Concurrency | `_CONCURRENCY` trigger variable | `80` |
60	| Max instances | `_MAX_INSTANCES` trigger variable | `10` |
61	| Auth | `--allow-unauthenticated` | Public (no Cloud IAM auth) |
62	
63	Runtime environment variables are not injected into the frontend container — all config is baked in at build time.
64	
65	## Environments

file.content:
## Docker image

**Build stages:**

1. **Builder** (`node:lts`):
   - `WORKDIR /app`
   - `yarn install --frozen-lockfile --production=false`
   - `yarn test` — **tests must pass or the build fails**
   - `yarn build:${NODE_ENV}` — Angular build with environment substitution
   - Output: `dist/browser/`

2. **Runtime** (`macbre/nginx-brotli:1.19.10`):
   - Copies `nginx.conf`
   - Copies `dist/browser/` to `/usr/share/nginx/html/`
   - Exposes port 80
   - Brotli-enabled nginx serves the SPA

**Image tag format:**
```
{REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/{PROJECT_ID}/{ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/{APP_ENV}/{APP_NAME}:{BUILD_ID}-{COMMIT_SHA}
```

Example:
```
us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/designflow/production/designflow-frontend:abc123-d4e5f6a7
```

## Cloud Run service

The image is deployed to Google Cloud Run (managed platform, not GKE). Cloud Run scales to zero when idle. Configuration:

| Parameter | Controlled by | Default (from cloudbuild.yaml) |
|---|---|---|
| CPU | `_CPU` trigger variable | `1` |
| Memory | `_MEMORY` trigger variable | `512Mi` |
| Concurrency | `_CONCURRENCY` trigger variable | `80` |
| Max instances | `_MAX_INSTANCES` trigger variable | `10` |
| Auth | `--allow-unauthenticated` | Public (no Cloud IAM auth) |

Runtime environment variables are not injected into the frontend container — all config is baked in at build time.

## Environments

input.old_string:
2. **Runtime** (`macbre/nginx-brotli:1.19.10`):
   - Copies `nginx.conf`

2. **Runtime** (`macbre/nginx-brotli:1.19.10`):
   - Copies `nginx.conf`
   - Copies `dist/browser/` to `/usr/share/nginx/html/`
   - Exposes port 80
   - Brotli-enabled nginx serves the SPA

## Static asset caching (nginx)

Caching policy lives in `nginx.conf`. The rule that matters:

- **`index.html` must never be long-cached.** It is the SPA entry point that references the build's content-hashed JS/CSS; if browsers cache it, they keep running an old app after a deploy. It is served `Cache-Control: no-cache` via an exact-match `location = /index.html` block (the browser stores it but revalidates every load → cheap 304s, instant pickup of new builds). Security headers (`X-Frame-Options`, etc.) are restated inside that block because an `add_header` in a location replaces the inherited http-level headers.
- **Content-hashed assets (`.js`, `.css`, images, `woff2`) are long-cached** — their filenames change every build, so this is safe.
- Do **not** add `html` back to the long-cache (`expires 30d`) bucket. Doing so re-creates the 2026-06-15 incident (stale frontend → empty RFQ sub-grids after a backend rollout). See `AGENTS.md §14`.

Note: a browser that cached `index.html` *before* this fix deployed will still honor its old 30-day cache until it expires or the user hard-refreshes once; the fix prevents recurrence going forward.

## Serve locally

```bash
npm run start
```

This serves against `environment.ts` (localhost:5004 API by default). The dev server runs on `http://localhost:4200` with live reload. The `--max-old-space-size=8192` flag is set in the script — the build is memory-heavy.

To serve against the sandbox backend instead of a local one, temporarily change `environment.ts` to use the `environment.sandbox.ts` URLs. Revert before committing.

## Build locally

```bash
# Against production API
npm run build:production

# Against sandbox API (Albert's environment)
npm run build:sandbox-albert
```

Output lands in `dist/browser/`. The production build uses output hashing (cache-busting filenames).

On Windows PowerShell, the package scripts' Unix-style `NODE_ENV=...` prefix is not parsed. Use the equivalent command directly:

```powershell
$env:NODE_ENV='sandbox-albert'
node --max_old_space_size=8192 ./node_modules/@angular/cli/bin/ng build --output-hashing=all --configuration sandbox
```

## Run tests

input.old_string: To serve against the sandbox backend instead of a local one, temporarily change `environment.ts` to use the `environment.sandbox.ts` URLs. Revert before committing.
input.new_string:
To serve against the **deployed sandbox backend** (real data, no local backend stack), do **not** just point `environment.ts` at the absolute `https://api.alsand.designflow.app` URLs — the sandbox BFF reflects a CORS allowlist that does **not** include `http://localhost:4200`, so browser calls fail with `HTTP 0` / "blocked by CORS policy" (verified 2026-06-15). Instead use a dev-server proxy so the browser talks same-origin and Angular forwards server-side:

1. Create `proxy.conf.json` (git-ignore / don't commit):
   ```json
   { "/api": { "target": "https://api.alsand.designflow.app", "secure": true, "changeOrigin": true } }
   ```
2. Temporarily set `environment.ts` to **relative** paths: `url: '/api/core'`, `tracking: '/api/tracking'`, `item_master: '/api/item_master'`, `data_sync: '/api/data_sync'`.
3. Run `ng serve --proxy-config proxy.conf.json --port 4200`.
4. Microsoft SSO works on localhost (the `localhost:4200` redirect URIs are registered — see `deployment.md`). Revert `environment.ts` and delete `proxy.conf.json` before committing.
